items.q: What does the dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia state?
items.correct: Positive symptoms are linked to excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways.
items.misconception: Schizophrenia is caused by too little dopamine.
items.random_wrong: Schizophrenia is caused by too much serotonin.
items.why: Students reverse the direction. The original hypothesis was HYPERdopaminergia. The revised version notes hypoactivity in prefrontal areas for negative symptoms, but excess in mesolimbic for positive.

items.q: According to Maslow, when can self-actualisation be achieved?
items.correct: Only after lower deficiency needs are largely met.
items.misconception: Anyone can self-actualise at any time through willpower.
items.why: Students miss the hierarchical structure. You cannot pursue growth needs while physiological, safety, love, and esteem needs are unmet - that's the whole point of a hierarchy.

items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: How long does information typically last in short-term memory without rehearsal, according to Peterson and Peterson?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 18 seconds. “30 seconds” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Whose 1932 'War of the Ghosts' study demonstrated reconstructive memory?
items.why: It's Bartlett. “Atkinson” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Atkinson and Shiffrin propose the multi-store model?
items.why: The real figure is 1968 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
